content.problemStatement:
There are NNN cars (numbered 111 through NNN) on a circular track with length NNN. For each iii (2≤i≤N2 \le i \le N2≤i≤N), the iii-th of them is at a distance i−1i-1i−1 clockwise from car 111, i.e. car 111 needs to travel a distance i−1i-1i−1 clockwise to reach car iii. Also, for each valid iii, the iii-th car has fif_ifi​ litres of gasoline in it initially.

You are driving car 111 in the clockwise direction. To move one unit of distance in this direction, you need to spend 111 litre of gasoline. When you pass another car (even if you'd run out of gasoline exactly at that point), you steal all its gasoline. Once you do not have any gasoline left, you stop.

What is the total clockwise distance travelled by your car?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of cars. The second line contains N space‑separated integers f1,f2,…,fN, where fi is the amount of gasoline (in litres) initially present in the i‑th car.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the total clockwise distance travelled by car 1 before it runs out of gasoline.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: start with 3 litres, can reach cars 2‑5 before fuel runs out, total distance 3.\nTest case 2: each car gives exactly 1 litre, allowing the car to move one unit, steal 1 litre, and repeat; after collecting the last litre it moves the final unit, total distance 5.\nTest case 3: after stealing all gasoline the car has 11 litres left at position 4, so it travels the remaining 11 units, total distance 15.
Only car 1 exists and it has no gasoline, so the car cannot move.
Fuel progression: start 2 → reach car2 (fuel 1+1) → reach car3 (fuel 1+4) → reach car4 (fuel 4) → no more cars, travel remaining 4 units. Total distance = 1+1+1+4 = 7.

solution.approach: Car 1 starts with f1 litres. While we have fuel we try to reach the next car in order (i = 2 … N). The distance to the i‑th car from the current position equals (i‑1) – distanceAlreadyTravelled. If the remaining fuel is at least this distance we can reach the car: we spend the needed fuel, increase the travelled distance, and add fi litres to the tank (stealing the gasoline). If the fuel is insufficient we move as far as possible (fuel units) and stop. After processing all cars (or stopping early) any leftover fuel is used to travel further on the empty track. The algorithm is a straightforward O(N) simulation per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the fuel amounts (can be O(1) if read on the fly).

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        f = [0] + [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        fuel = f[1]
        dist = 0
        for i in range(2, N+1):
            need = (i-1) - dist
            if fuel >= need:
                fuel -= need
                dist += need
                fuel += f[i]
            else:
                dist += fuel
                fuel = 0
                break
        dist += fuel
        out_lines.append(str(dist))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
